steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage states a proposal — fracture toughness rises with cobalt proportion — then says the data show this is not the case. The blank needs a specific example from the table that disproves the proposal. A counterexample must show that changing cobalt proportion does not produce the expected change in fracture toughness.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — cobalt constitutes the same proportion of HEA 7 as it does of HEA 95.: if two alloys have equal cobalt content but different fracture toughness (as the table shows), cobalt proportion alone cannot explain the difference — the proposal is disproved. ✓
  • Choice B — cobalt constitutes a higher proportion of HEA 15 than it does of HEA 7.: a difference in cobalt proportion between two alloys could still be consistent with the proposal if HEA 15 also has higher toughness. This doesn’t establish a counterexample. ✗
  • Choice C — cobalt constitutes a substantial proportion of HEA 15 but does not constitute any of HEA 51.: same problem as B — differing cobalt levels might still correlate with differing toughness, leaving the proposal unchallenged. ✗
  • Choice D — cobalt constitutes a different proportion of HEA 25 than it does of HEA 7.: again, a difference in cobalt proportion cannot by itself refute the proposal without knowing whether toughness moved as predicted. ✗
That matches Choice A — cobalt constitutes the same proportion of HEA 7 as it does of HEA 95..
